posts.title: Auschwitz Tickets Online Only in 2026: What Changed

children.text: Since March 1, 2026, entry cards to the Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial are available exclusively online at
. The Museum ended all on-site ticket sales to combat unethical tour operators who misled visitors into paying inflated prices.
Why No More On-Site Ticket Sales
How to Use Official Reservation System
Why the Museum Ended On-Site Ticket Sales

For years, unethical tour operators exploited the on-site ticket system at the Memorial. They fabricated stories about sold-out dates to pressure visitors into purchasing overpriced packages — sometimes at several times the actual cost.

The problem escalated to the point where visitors queued from 3–4 AM to secure entry cards, leading to conflicts. The Museum received a growing number of complaints about misleading practices and inflated prices charged by third-party operators.

Even if entry cards were still available, visitors sometimes had to wait several hours for their tour to begin. We also documented heated conflicts among some ‘tour operators’ over their spots in line, which required intervention by the Museum Security and even the Police. Thanks to the changes being introduced, such situations will no longer occur.

media.alt: On-site ticket sales available at the Auschwitz I Main Camp entrance.

children.text: On-site entry card sales at Auschwitz I, before the system was discontinued in March 2026.

platform is the only official reservation system for the Memorial. Visitors can reserve entry cards up to three months in advance. Free individual entry cards become available seven days before each visit date. Guided tour tickets are released progressively and remain available until sold out.
Same-day booking is possible. The system displays real-time availability, so visitors can check open time slots directly from their mobile device — even on the way to the Memorial.
This means that even when planning a last-minute trip to the Museum, it will still be possible to check real-time availability for guided tours and to book them immediately.

media.alt: Scanning tickets before entering Auschwitz Memorial

children.text: Scanning entry card at the Memorial entrance - ID is required!
3 Rules to Avoid Auschwitz Ticket Scams
This is the sole official platform. Entry cards purchased elsewhere carry no Museum guarantee.
Ignore "sold out" claims from third parties.
Operators routinely fabricate scarcity to push expensive packages. Always check availability yourself on the official site.
If you encounter websites or agencies making false claims about ticket availability, report them to the Museum directly.
More on avoiding Auschwitz ticket scams
Book your entry cards only at

This is the only official system for reserving entry cards to visit the site of the former German Nazi concentration and extermination camp Auschwitz. The Museum does not cooperate in this regard with any external entities and bears no responsibility for reservations made or cancelled on other websites.

posts.title: How to Get from Kraków to Auschwitz

children.text: The Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial is in Oświęcim, about 70 km west of Kraków. Most visitors travel from Kraków — and there are several reliable ways to get there. To navigate on site and locate yourself open the custom map using the button in the bottom right.
From Kraków to Auschwitz by Train: the Fastest Way

. The journey takes between 40 minutes on an InterCity express and 1 to 1.5 hours on a regional train. There are two routes (via Trzebinia or Skawina) with several stations in Kraków along the way, where you can also board, and tickets can be purchased on the train, preferably right after boarding. Check connections at:
From Oświęcim Station to the Memorial
The train station in Oświęcim is 1.5 km from Auschwitz I (where you start the tour) and 1.5 km from Auschwitz II-Birkenau (where you finish). You can walk (about 25 minutes) or

media.alt: Train station and the bus stop in front of it in Oswiecim

children.text: The train station in Oświęcim with the "Dworzec PKP" bus stop in front.
The most convenient option is the
, which runs directly from Kraków to the Auschwitz Memorial. It drops you off at the main parking lot, right next to the Memorial entrance — no transfers, no walking from a distant station.
Lajkonik buses leave Kraków roughly every hour between 7 a.m. and 3 p.m. The trip takes about 1.5 hours. Tickets cost 22 PLN one way — pay on board with cash or card, or reserve online at
When you book through Flixbus, you only pay an additional booking fee, but you’ll be traveling on the Lajkonik bus.
Lajkonik departs from stands G1–G6 at the
, next to Kraków Główny railway station. Timetables:

media.alt: Inside the Lajkonik bus heading from Kraków toward Oświęcim — Auschwitz Memorial.

children.text: Inside the Lajkonik bus heading from Kraków toward Oświęcim — a comfortable, direct connection to the Memorial.
The bus also stops along the route — useful if your accommodation is outside the centre (links to Google Maps):
Plan your trip to the nearest stop with
Getting from Kraków to Auschwitz by Car: Distance, Time, Routes & Parking
The drive from Kraków to Auschwitz takes just 1 to 1.5 hours (70 km), making it an easy and popular day trip. Here are the three best routes depending on where you’re starting in Kraków:
– the fastest paid option. Ideal if you’re leaving from north of the city. Take the exit toward Oświęcim near Chrzanów for the quickest route.
– via Liszki and Alwernia. May be quicker when departing from Kraków city center, especially during heavy traffic.
– through Skawina and Zator. The best choice if you’re starting from the south of Kraków.

Paid parking lots are available at both Auschwitz I and Auschwitz II-Birkenau. The main parking lot at Auschwitz I is located on Więźniów Oświęcimia Street and stays open until closing time. For detailed parking maps, current prices, and alternative lots,

media.alt: Road sign on the way from Kraków to Oświęcim showing the distance.

children.text: On the way from Kraków look for road signs showing 'Oświęcim', and closer to the Memorial also 'Muzeum Auschwitz'.
Get to Auschwitz From the Kraków Airport
, take the train to Kraków Główny (20 minutes) and connect from there by train or bus to Oświęcim. There is no direct connection from the airport.
Make sure to reserve your entry pass a few weeks before coming — since March 2026, tickets are available only online at visit.auschwitz.org.

posts.title: Krakow to Auschwitz Day Trip: Complete Planning Guide

children.text: Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial is the most visited site from Krakow—and for good reason. At just 70 km away, it is an accessible day trip. But planning carefully makes all the difference between a rushed experience and a meaningful one. Here is a step-by-step guide from a licensed Auschwitz educator.
Planning Your Day: A Realistic Timeline
Auschwitz day trip from Kraków takes about 8 hours: 4 hours travelling between the cities and 4 hours sightseeing including
drive from Auschwitz I to Birkenau
. Here is a realistic schedule that works for most visitors:
— Depart Kraków by bus or train.
— Arrive in Oświęcim, get to the Memorial and
(allowed 30 min before the entry time on your ticket).
— Wait for your your guide in the Visitor Service Center or begin the tour of Auschwitz I on your own with a free entry pass.
— Take the free shuttle bus to Auschwitz II-Birkenau (runs several times per hour - the guide explains exactly where it is, when you depart and how you meet again).
— Finish visiting Birkenau and get to Oświęcim train station (30 min walk) or back to Auschwitz I for the bus.

media.alt: Reception entrance at Auschwitz I.

children.text: Allow at least 30 minutes before your tour slot for security check.
3 Ways to Get There From Kraków
A detailed post with transport guidelines

Lajkonik buses depart from the MDA bus station (Małopolskie Dworce Autobusowe) next to Kraków Główny train station. The ride takes about 1.5 hours and costs around 22 PLN one way. Buses drop you off right at the Memorial entrance — this is the easiest option.

Trains run from Kraków Główny to Oświęcim station. The journey takes about 1 hour and costs around 18 PLN. The downside: Oświęcim station is about 1.5 km from the Memorial, so you will need to walk or take a local bus for the last stretch.

media.alt: Train station and the bus stop in front of it in Oswiecim

children.text: Oświęcim train station — local buses to the Memorial depart from here.

Take the A4 motorway west from Krakow. The drive takes about 1 hour in normal traffic (or 1.5 hours avoiding motorway fee). Parking at the Memorial costs 20 PLN. This option gives you the most flexibility with timing.
What to Book Before You Go
at least 2–3 weeks ahead (90-day window). Choose between a free afternoon pass or a paid guided tour. Since March 2026, all passes are online-only — no on-site sales.
Bus tickets can be bought on board, but reserving at moj-bus.pl guarantees your seat in summer.
Check bus and train schedules for return. Last buses back fill up in peak season, however return tickets are not mandatory as it is very easy to come back by train. You may also decide last minute to
What to Bring for the Day
(printed or on your phone) and a
— both are checked at the entrance.
— there are limited food options near the Memorial.
— you will cover several kilometres on uneven ground.
— Birkenau is an open-air site with no shelter.
— maximum 30 x 20 x 10 cm. Larger bags must be left in storage.
— required if joining a guided tour (audio receivers use this connector).
For more on what to wear and what is not allowed, see the

media.alt: Luggage storage with grey lockers and a payment machine at Auschwitz I main parking lot.

children.text: Lockers near the entrance — bags over 30×20×10 cm must be stored here.
Day Trip Costs at a Glance
Here is what a day trip to Auschwitz typically costs per person:
Total for a self-guided visit by bus:
Total for a guided visit by bus:
More ticket prices and tour options
— since 2026, there are no on-site sales. Reserve online.
— allow at least 30 minutes before your tour slot for security screening.
Combining Auschwitz with the Salt Mines
— possible but exhausting. Groups arriving at the same time cause long queues at both sites.
— last buses fill up quickly, especially in summer.
— temperatures drop below zero and most of the visit is outdoors.
— anything over 30×20×10 cm must go to lockers, which means extra time at security.

Give yourself quiet time. This is not a place you leave and immediately move on from. Many visitors find it difficult to process the experience immediately. The bus or train ride back to Krakow provides natural decompression time.
If you have energy left in the evening, consider a quiet walk through Kazimierz — Krakow's historic Jewish quarter. It offers a different but complementary perspective on Jewish history and culture.

children.text: Auschwitz Tickets & Tours: How to Book
Free Entry, Guided Tours & Private Guide Options

meta.title: Auschwitz Tickets: Official Booking & Tour Types
meta.description: Step-by-step instructions for booking official entry passes online. Compare tour types, check current prices, and avoid long queues with a licensed guide.

children.text: Entry to the Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial is completely free in the afternoon, but you must reserve a named, timed-entry pass in advance exclusively on the official website
visit.auschwitz.org — book at least 2–3 weeks ahead, as availability is limited and fills up quickly,
For a more structured experience, guided group tours (with an official Museum educator) cost about 150 PLN per person and typically last 3.5 hours.
How to Book Auschwitz Tickets in 2026?
a scheduled guided group tour for a fee (check

The Auschwitz Memorial does not cooperate with any third-party booking platforms. These companies use the same public booking system and often cancel tours at the last minute. However, if you're looking for
with transportation, this can only be arranged through external websites or apps.
Practical Tips Before Booking Auschwitz Tickets

accordionItems.question: Are Auschwitz Tickets Online Only in 2026?

children.text: Yes. From March 1, 2026, all entry passes to the Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial must be booked online. On-site ticket sales have been permanently discontinued.

Book your free individual entry or guided tour exclusively through the official museum website. Be cautious of third-party sites charging inflated prices — the official booking system is the only legitimate source.

accordionItems.question: What to Do If Your Auschwitz Tour Was Canceled?

children.text: Cancellations typically happen when the booking wasn’t made directly with the Museum. Your first step should be to check the official system at
If no tours are available, try local tour providers — though prices may be high, and availability limited.

accordionItems.question: Which Documents Are Required to Enter the Muzeum?

children.text: (passport, driver's license, etc.) is required and must be shown before scanning your entry pass at the Auschwitz Memorial entrance.
(printed or digital with a visible barcode) is scanned before security and again at the reception area.
Can You Visit Auschwitz Without a Guide?
A maximum of ten people can enter the Auschwitz Museum without a guide, but only in the afternoon. Entrance is free, but a pass is still required. Be sure to read also
the comparison to a guided tour

accordionItems.question: How to Book Your Free Auschwitz Entry Pass?

children.text: To visit Auschwitz on your own, you must reserve an individual entry pass online, at least 7 days in advance. Go to
Visit for individuals -> select a date -> Tour for individuals without an educator
These afternoon slots are listed without any language assigned. If none are visible, they’re likely fully booked for the day—during high season, this may be the case for several weeks ahead.

accordionItems.question: What to Do on the Day of Your Tour?

children.text: page and once there you follow these steps:
Present your ID and scan the entry pass
Scan your ticket again to go downstairs
Get outside of the tunnel and to
Show your pass and go inside

accordionItems.question: How to Visit Auschwitz on Your Own?

children.text: Outdoors, use our map to find your location. Indoors, follow a guidebook and move in the same direction as the guided groups. Without a historical background, the place may not give you much, so consider a guided tour first.
Open my custom map with Auschwitz tour routes (the button in the bottom right corner) and locate yourself on site.
Guidebooks come in many languages and contain the regular sightseeing route with commentaries. You can get it at the bookshop inside
What Are Auschwitz Guided Tours Like?
Most visitors choose organized tours for their convenience. However, to avoid last-minute cancellations, it's best to book a tour arranged by the Museum through visit.auschwitz.org.

accordionItems.question: Tours Arranged by the Museum - 3 Options

children.text: (called 'visit for individuals') is ideal for single visitors who haven't made a reservation in advance. For groups over 6 people it's cheaper to get a private tour.
(called 'visit for groups') with your own guide is for demanding and larger groups. It's convenient and flexible, with more languages available.
(called 'online group visit') is designed for people who cannot visit the Memorial in person. It may be a good choice for schools because of the additional educational content.

accordionItems.question: Organized Auschwitz Tour

children.text: with transportation from Kraków is the most popular option. Check reviews, as quality can vary, and be aware of the potential
A private tour booked directly with the Museum may be cheaper for groups of several people.

accordionItems.question: Guided Tour Types

children.text: You visit both parts of the camp and receive basic information.
You can visit more than on a 3.5-h tour: national exhibitions,
... You can choose between a one-day option (6/8 hours) or a two-day option (3+3 or 4+4 hours).
You visit less than on 3.5-hour tour at the same price. Choose 3.5-h instead.
You join a live video broadcast of the Memorial, led by a guide.
Headset. It's Not an Audio Guide.

Visitors are provided with a headset for listening to the guide’s narration. For comfort, bring your own headphones (3.5mm audio jack) and ensure they’re on only after the guide begins speaking. If the sound quality is poor, ask for a replacement.
Adjust the volume using the small knob as needed.
How to Join a Guided Auschwitz Tour?
Join a group of up to 30 people on a 3.5- or 6-hour tour with a fixed itinerary. For a better experience, booking
is recommended. It offers a wider selection of languages and can be more cost-effective for families or groups—check the prices below.

accordionItems.question: How to Book a Guided Tour for Individual Visitors?

children.text: Visit for individuals -> select a date -> select language -> select the visiting hour -> add to basket
In March 2026, on-site ticket sales were stopped.

accordionItems.question: Which Languages Are Available and Is There an Audio Guide?

children.text: Guided tours for individual visitors are available in English, French, German, Italian, Polish, Russian, and Spanish.
There is no audioguide system at Auschwitz Museum, but you will use
to hear your guide clearly during the tour.

accordionItems.question: What Are the Ticket Prices for Joining an Auschwitz Guided Tour?

children.text: 3.5-h regular tour (for groups over 6 people it is cheaper to
is available to students under 26, people over 75 or with a disability card.
In Polish the sightseeing is 10-20 PLN cheaper apart from the online tours.

accordionItems.question: When to Arrive and What to Do on the Day of Your Visit?

children.text: Get there at least half an hour before your tour. For directions check the
page and once there you follow these steps:
Present your ID and entry pass (f you lose your ticket, ask the staff for assistance with reprinting it on site)
Get a special sticker at the front desk (details and photos below)
Wait for your guide in the hallway
Scan the ticket and go downstairs
Get a headset to hear your guide
Kraków to Auschwitz Day Trip Guide
After passing through the security gates, go to the reception desk to get a sticker that will help the guide find you. Wait for the guide holding the language board in the hallway.
Is a Private Auschwitz Tour Worth It?
If you hire a guide you can choose which places to visit and what to explain in detail. You can start 30 min early, but you lose time if late.

accordionItems.question: How and When Can I Book a Private Auschwitz Guided Tour?

children.text: select the 'Visit for groups' option. After registering and submitting your request, you’ll receive a response by email.

It’s best to book at least one month in advance. Group bookings are available up to 5 days before the visit, or up to 2 days in advance for an additional fee by contacting: 'reservation.office@auschwitz.org'
Book early and inform the Museum of any special requests—such as a custom route, planned ceremonies, or visitors with special needs.

children.text: The prices* for  groups of up to

accordionItems.question: Which Languages Are Offered for the Private Sightseeing?

children.text: Private guided tours are available in: Croatian, Czech, Dutch, English, French, German, Greek, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Polish, Russian, Serbian, Slovak, Spanish, Swedish, Ukrainian.

accordionItems.question: When to Arrive and What to Do on Site?

children.text: Arrive at least 40 minutes early so that you can use restrooms, in the meantime the group leader should check in at
(see photo below) to meet your guide
Everyone presents their ID and entry passes (distribute tickets
The group gathers in the reception hallway

Those who hire a guide should ask at this reception desk.  It is located to the right of the Memorial entrance.
Are Organized Auschwitz Tours from Krakow Worth It?
Auschwitz tours are available through hotel receptions and apps, but intermediaries often add a commission, and
To avoid extra costs and ensure your visit, book directly with the trusted local operators listed below.

accordionItems.question: Description of a Typical Auschwitz Day Trip from Kraków

children.text: Visiting Auschwitz with transport from Kraków takes at least six hours:
Pick-up in Kraków (may take a while)
Receive your tickets and wait for your guide
Present your ID, scan your ticket, and proceed through the security screening
The group gathers inside; tickets are scanned again
Guided tour of Auschwitz I (around 2 hours)
Short break, then return to the bus for transfer to Birkenau
Return to the bus and drive back to Kraków (around 1.5 hours)
In this post I explain how you can organize a day trip from Kraków yourself.
Verified Local Suppliers of Auschwitz Tours
A reliable company offering high-quality sightseeing experiences with direct customer service.
Guided tours in English, with optional guidebooks in French, German, Italian, and Spanish.
Trusted and award-winning, uniquely offering the exclusive tour capped at 15 visitors.
Explore Auschwitz and beyond with expert guides and safe, comfortable transport.
Last-minute bookings with a native British tour agent from Kraków. WhatsApp: +48 732 914 971

Some companies (other than those listed above) cancel bookings at the last minute or change departure times. Negative reviews are often quickly removed from platforms like Get Your Guide, so always check the operator’s reviews on Google or TripAdvisor – they are reliable. The following information comes from my Facebook group, where you can find support from people who have already visited the Memorial:

accordionItems.question: Why Can Auschwitz Tour Bookings Be Canceled?

children.text: Museum guides can be booked up to 90 days in advance, while tour companies may charge at any time. Sometimes, guides are requested only after the entry fee is paid, which might not be possible. You may also be replaced at the last minute by someone willing to pay more for your spot, causing the tour to be canceled—even the day before.

accordionItems.question: Are There Cheaper Auschwitz Tours and What Are the Risks?

children.text: Prices for organized Auschwitz tours usually range from 160 to 300 PLN per person. Booking in advance is cheaper, and can be as low as 40 PLN for special offers but these tours are often cancelled as explained above. Scammers try to sell free tickets or fail to explain that you are only paying for transportation.

accordionItems.question: What Causes Crowds at the Auschwitz Memorial?

children.text: Combining Auschwitz tour with a visit to Wieliczka Salt Mine, many companies arrive at the same time. Hence the long lines and crowds at the exhibition in the morning and early afternoon.

accordionItems.question: How Tiring Can a Visit to Auschwitz Be?

children.text: Companies try to gather as large a group as possible, so you usually spend half a day surrounded by 30 strangers. Imagine being picked up in jammed Kraków, a long drive, queues, and rushing around. Moreover, visiting Auschwitz and the Salt Mine is physically demanding, so it’s not advisable to combine both tours on the same day.

accordionItems.question: Is Visiting Auschwitz With a Big Group a Waste of Time?

children.text: The 30 people who see the place for the first time have to gather several times. During the breaks, some try to use the crowded restrooms or eat something in the crowded restaurant. What the guide says often needs to be translated, which reduces the available time for narration even by half.

accordionItems.question: How Can a Tight Schedule Affect Your Auschwitz Visit?

children.text: A busy schedule can make you feel rushed. Fixed itinerary only allows you to visit certain places, missing out on so much. It is much better to
go there yourself and visit the places you want at your own pace.
How to Get to Auschwitz Museum
By Bus, Train, Car or Organized Transfer

meta.title: How to Get to Auschwitz: Transport & Parking Guide
meta.description: All transport options to the Auschwitz Memorial: trains, buses, driving routes, and parking. Compare travel times and costs to plan a smooth arrival.

children.text: The Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial is located in Oświęcim and can be reached from many Polish cities by bus, train, car, or private transfer. Most visitors come from Kraków (a distance of just 70 km / 1–1.5 hours’ drive).
Keep in mind that the tour covers two parts of the former camp located in different places—details below.
Do not travel immediately before the tour, which is physically demanding in itself. It is recommended to find accommodation nearby and visit Auschwitz after a good night’s sleep.
Check out How to Get There
The sightseeing begins at Auschwitz I — check the map using the button in the bottom right corner.
The second part of the tour takes place at Auschwitz II-Birkenau, 2 km away — see below for how to get there.
How to Get from Auschwitz I to Birkenau?
Whether by tour bus or car, you'll arrive at the
and walk a few minutes to the main gate.
includes a free shuttle between Auschwitz and Birkenau.
The 8-minute ride departs every 12 minutes. Check timetables at bus stops.
Taxis are located near the above bus stops. Some prices are fixed:
From Kraków to Auschwitz by Bus, Train or Car

Most visitors come to the Museum in Oświęcim from Kraków as the cities are well connected. Below is a summary of the most popular arrival methods with links to details, farther down you’ll find more general tips, such as where to park near Auschwitz and how to get there from the train station, regardless of where you’re coming from.
In 1.5 hours the Lajkonik bus will take you directly to the entrance of the Memorial. You must book your ticket in advance, as the bus departs only a few times a day.
Check the schedules, booking details, and stops.
Some trains from Kraków reach Oświęcim in as little as 40 minutes—
. Trains run even late at night, but the station in Oświęcim is located about 1.5 km from the Memorial—
The drive takes about an hour,
depending on the route you choose
. This option offers the most flexibility, but keep in mind that sightseeing can be tiring.
Parking near the Memorial costs around 20 PLN.
Private Transfer to Auschwitz: What Options Are Available?

Cab drivers and private transport companies can take you to Auschwitz from almost anywhere. Determine the exact price before your trip and let the service provider know that they can leave contact information here.
Click the map icon on the right and select “Parking lots” in the upper right corner to see the nearest parking options.
Best routes from Kraków are here
. The links below show the locations on Google Maps.
You can stay only till the closing time for (PLN):
Do not leave cars in bus spaces.
Facilities at the main parking lot.

accordionItems.question: More Parking Spaces near Auschwitz

children.text: It is forbidden to park on the opposite side of the street within the housing estate, but the free lot shown below is 700 meters away. More parking, a supermarket, hotels and restaurants are within 1 km. You can also use Park & Ride near
The nearest lot is only 400 m from the main Birkenau gate but is expensive. There are restrooms, shops and a restaurant.
The small lot 700 m from Birkenau entrance is cheaper (PLN):
Get to Auschwitz by Bus: Schedules, Prices & Stops
Oświęcim can be reached from nearby towns and cities (
is the most popular choice). All intercity buses pass through
. Here are a few carriers I’m familiar with:
From Bielsko-Biała for 20 PLN (the bus stops right next to the Auschwitz Museum;
From Głębowice for 9 PLN (last stop is 100 m away from the Memorial;
Discover significant historical sites like former Auschwitz sub-camps or the synagogue and castle near
Can You Get to Auschwitz from Kraków by Train?

Yes, and from many other Polish cities, however Kraków offers a fast, direct connection. The train station in Oświęcim is 1.5 km from Auschwitz and 1.5 km from Birkenau. Go to the Museum by bus, cab or walk (20 min). Check the train timetables at:
Public Buses Between the Train Station and Auschwitz I
from the "Dworzec PKP" stop in front of the station. After 4 minutes, get off at the "Muzeum I" stop and walk 200 m further down the road to the Memorial entrance.
Check nearest departures  on the public transport website:
(all buses go back to the station)
The bus stop 'Muzeum II' on Obozowa Street. 'Muzeum I' is behind the grocery store on the left –
Which Airports Are Closest to Auschwitz?
Kraków has excellent transport links to Oświęcim

, which is why most people choose Balice Airport to get to the Museum. Katowice is geographically closer, but the nearby Pyrzowice Airport is roughly the same distance away, about 60 km. Unfortunately, there are no direct connections between these airports and Auschwitz.
How to Get from Airports in Kraków and Katowice to Auschwitz?

Upon arrival at Balice, take the train to Kraków Główny (20 minutes), and from there, transfer to a train or bus to Oświęcim. You can also reach the train station by public transportation, but the trip takes 40 minutes—check the schedule at
The best option is to go to the train station in Katowice and
; however, there are no direct connections, so you’ll have to transfer in Trzebinia, Mysłowice, or Czechowice-Dziedzice. There is a direct Flixbus but only one per day, in the morning.
Auschwitz Memorial: What to Expect on Site
Entry, Facilities, Opening Hours & Nearby Sites

meta.title: Auschwitz Entry: Opening Hours, Tickets & Security
meta.description: Entry guidelines: opening times, security checks, reception desk and facility maps. Explore related sites and museums around the Memorial camp.

children.text: The Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial comprises two sites 3.5 km apart—Auschwitz I and Auschwitz II-Birkenau—linked by a free shuttle bus. It is open daily except 1 January, 25 December, and Easter Sunday, with airport-style security at the entrance. Plan at least 3–4 hours to visit both sites; facilities include restrooms and a restaurant, and nearby you can see related memorials or explore Oświęcim town.
What to Expect at the Auschwitz Visitor Center?
I took this photo at the entrance to Auschwitz (to see it on map use the button in the bottom right). It's the first thing you'll see when you arrive. The main parking lot has restrooms and
On the right side, there are snack vending machines, tourist info, and an ATM. Further back, you’ll find
stop, a restaurant, and a hostel.
What Are the Opening Hours of the Auschwitz Memorial?
Entrance hours depend on the month and whether you visit with or without a guide.
The Memorial is closed on January 1, December 25, Easter Sunday, and occasionally for events.
on the map mark the locations shown in the photo below:
How to Enter Auschwitz Memorial Site?
Long lines may be for groups—check these
At entry, show your ticket and ID. No need for ID afterward, but hold onto your ticket.
Put metal items in a blue container and walk through the scanner, like at airports.
After security, wait for your guide or group in the hallway. Toilets are on the left, and an info desk is in the middle.
The info desk offers assistance, lost ticket printing, and
Nearby doors lead to a bookshop, café, and tourist info.
The tourist info office offers free Wi-Fi, computers, and city guidebooks. More on Oświęcim at
Scan your ticket one last time
at the hallway’s end, then go downstairs. No ID needed, but keep your ticket for
On a guided tour, you receive a
A brief tunnel walk takes you outside.
By the trees ahead, take a left to the

Oświęcim, with over 800 years of history, offers more than Auschwitz. Explore its rich heritage, beautiful surroundings, and vibrant culture.
5 Important Memorial Sites Near Auschwitz Worth Visiting
Foundation of Memory Sites Near Auschwitz-Birkenau
The foundation protects artifacts and remains  of former Auschwitz sub-camps through conservation efforts and exhibitions.
Art Exhibition by an Auschwitz Survivor

"Clichés of Memory – Labyrinths" is a permanent art exhibition by Marian Kołodziej. He studied graphics and painting, but it wasn't until 40 years later that he chose to share his camp memories, transforming them into haunting visual narratives.
Remembrance Museum of Land of Oświęcim Residents

The museum honors the local inhabitants who risked their lives to aid Auschwitz prisoners during World War II. Through multimedia exhibits, visitors can experience the emotions and stories of these courageous individuals.
Visit the Oshpitzin Museum to explore the rich history of the local Jewish community and step inside the only surviving synagogue nearby.
Explore the Roma history and culture through exhibitions and educational programs that highlight their rich heritage and experiences.
Is Oświęcim Town Worth Visiting After Auschwitz?

The market square, synagogue and castle are all in one place. Stroll through the charming old town, admire its historic architecture, and experience the warm hospitality of a community dedicated to peace and remembrance.
Visit the medieval Oświęcim Castle to learn about the city's rich past through captivating exhibits in its historic chambers and mysterious underground.
Step back to the vibrant era of interwar Oświęcim at the Town Hall Museum. Experience the charm of the 1930s by getting behind the wheel of a vintage Praga Baby automobile.
What Facilities & Services Are Available at Auschwitz?
A visit to Auschwitz is demanding, but the nearby nature offers a place to recover. Find more on the official Małopolska Region tourism portal.
Rest well before your visit to remain focused. Accommodation options near the Museum include hotels, Airbnb stays, and integration centers.
The closest restaurants can be busy or expensive. Go right from the main car park gate for alternatives. Locations of my favorite fast food places:
A regular grocery store is 200 m from Auschwitz parking lot on Obozowa St., with more stores further down. See the location.
The integration centers below are just 1.5 km from the Auschwitz Memorial, offering a range of services, including accommodation.
The IYMC is a place of international and intergenerational meetings, a forum for the exchange of ideas and dialogue across cultural, social, and religious borders.
The center is a place of reconciliation between people of different cultures. Educational activities include meetings with former prisoners, conferences, and seminars.
Located between two rivers, Oświęcim is surrounded by lakes and forests. Some are so close you can get there on foot.
Leaving the main Auschwitz parking lot, turn left and walk 8 min.
To the west of Birkenau there are many quiet spots along the river.
In the villages to the south, you can relax by the numerous lakes.
Auschwitz Tour Routes: Both Sites Step by Step

meta.title: Auschwitz Tour Routes: Main Sites & Interactive Map
meta.description: Explore detailed routes for Auschwitz I and Birkenau. View interactive maps, essential locations, and insightful recommendations from a licensed guide.

children.text: The Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial consists of two distinct sites: Auschwitz I, featuring the "Arbeit Macht Frei" gate, indoor exhibitions, and the only preserved gas chamber; and Auschwitz II-Birkenau (3 km away), with the iconic selection ramp, barracks, and crematoria ruins. This guide provides detailed tour routes (3.5–6h) with custom maps, covering both major landmarks and lesser-known historical sites off the standard path for a deeper perspective.
How to Follow the Auschwitz Tour Route (50 Locations + Map)
are listed here in the in the order they appear on a standard sightseeing route. Use the interactive Auschwitz camp map and location list (found in the bottom-right corner) to navigate with ease.
Proceed to Auschwitz II-Birkenau, located 2 km away.
Check how to get to Birkenau.

accordionItems.question: ⚙️ Does the Auschwitz Tour Route Ever Change?

children.text: The 3.5-hour tour includes a visit to either block 6 or 7 in Auschwitz and either brick or wooden barracks in Birkenau. Specific location requests can be made.
Private tours may start at Birkenau if there are long entry lines at Auschwitz.

accordionItems.question: ☔ What Are the Auschwitz Tour Conditions and Regulations?

children.text: The tour involves several hours of walking on uneven, rocky, and muddy terrain, with no weather protection.
Eating and smoking are not allowed. Please silence your phone and refrain from using flash indoors.

accordionItems.question: 🚾 Where Are Toilets and Places to Rest on the Auschwitz Grounds?

children.text: restrooms are in block 18, next to block 7. Some buildings, like blocks 17 and 27, have benches and air conditioning to cool down on hot days.
restrooms are located to the right behind the gate. Another restroom and benches can be found in the wooded area to the right behind the monument.

Your tour begins near the main gate after a 10-minute walk from the reception and
behind the wooden building to the left.
To the right of the main gate, you can see an anti-aircraft bunker. Like the present gas chamber
it was built in 1944 against potential bombings.
Past the gate, the camp kitchen with a photo of the orchestra is on the right, and Block 24, once the camp brothel, is on the left.
Opened in 1943 in Block 24, the camp brothel forced women from Ravensbrück into prostitution. Only privileged prisoners were allowed access.
Prisoners’ orchestra rehearsals were held here; they performed at roll calls and SS events, gaining some protection from typical forced labor.
Prisoners with artistic skills worked here on drawings, signs, or propaganda for the SS. Some also created secret works as acts of resistance.
Young prisoners, trained in masonry, made the plaster samples (below) on block 14 (on the left in the photo above).
Visitors walking from the main gate towards block 4.
The remains of the first camp kitchen and bathhouse are between blocks 1 and 2.
The bathhouse, built in March 1942, had three heat chimneys, water cisterns, showers, and a sewer system.
SS doctors conducted selections there, sending sick prisoners deemed unfit for work to the gas chambers.
These buildings, preserved in their original condition, are only accessible on a 6-hour study tour.
According to German regulations, heaters had to be build in every concentration camp. They were installed all over the area, even though prisoners were not allowed to use them.
A typical tiled heater at Auschwitz I. Block 5 →
Remains of a double heater from a wooden barrack. →
Some heaters in brick barracks still bear the Volkswagen logo. →

On the right, you can see a photograph of Jews undergoing selection (you’ll walk past the wooden barrack later on).

Luggage confiscated from deportees, including eyeglasses, Jewish prayer shawls, items of people with disabilities, metal pots, as well as shoes and suitcases on the first floor.
The water reservoir behind block 6 at Auschwitz I resembles a swimming pool unlike
Prisoner registration (categories, tattoos, striped uniforms) and the basics of camp daily Routine – forced labor and life in the barracks.
The harsh realities of camp existence – exhaustion, hunger, cold, fear – along with the poor living and sanitary conditions.
Dr. Clauberg conducted sterilization experiments here. It cannot be entered, but you can take a
Between blocks 10 and 11, you can see the poles used for tortures. Hands were tied behind and the knot was hanged on the hook, so that arm muscles were slowly stretching.

On the ground floor, you see the summary court where Poles were sentenced to death in brief trials before being executed at the Death Wall; in the basement, you visit the internal prison, where inmates were tortured, including being held in standing cells.
Learn about the camp resistance in the hallway’s end. Read the famous:
Near block 11, the Zyklon B storage is on the left and the camp laundry on the right.
The camp post office is between blocks 25 and 26.
By the kitchen you can see the vegetable storage.
Opened in 2013, the Shoah exhibition by Yad Vashem showcases pre-war Jewish communities on the ground floor, where you can also find a vast book listing around 4 million Holocaust victims.
in Europe, German Nazi propaganda, testimonies of Holocaust survivors and reproductions of children's drawings reflecting their experiences.
Opened in 2005, the exhibition depicts the lives of Dutch citizens and Jewish communities before and during World War II.
In the so called hospitals prisoners selected by the SS as unable to work were killed on regular basis.
The exhibition, opened in 2005, presents biographies, photos and testimonies. Topics: exclusion, deportation, extermination, returns.
Opened in 2006, the exhibition highlights the German occupation, persecution of Jews, and deportations from Belgium.
Road rollers by block 18 were often pulled by the penal unit.
An unusual team for a road roller
Opened in 2004, the exhibition explains the situation of Jews in Hungary during World War II and the changes after March 1944.
The exhibition opened in 2021 depicts Austrians as prisoners and victims of Auschwitz, as well as perpetrators.
Opened in 2002, the exhibition traces the history of the Holocaust in Slovakia, detailing the persecution and deportations.
The exhibition opened in 2002 explains the mechanism of deportation from the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia.
Opened in 2025, this exhibition presents the fate of Poles in the German Nazi concentration camp Auschwitz. It also examines life in Oświęcim during the German occupation (1939–1945).
Opened in 2013, this exhibition explores the repression of Soviet civilians and prisoners of war. See more photos at:
The exhibition, which opened in 2001, depicts the persecution and genocide of the Roma in the Third Reich and occupied Europe.
Additional barbed wire was installed in 1944 around Blocks 22 and 23 (opposite Block 13) to divide women’s work groups from male prisoners. The same guard tower appears in the photo below.
On your way to the gas chamber, you will stop near the camp kitchen to learn more about roll calls and public executions. A reporting officer often stayed in the wooden booth.
Next to the gallows where R. Höss was hanged you enter Gas Chamber and Crematorium I. Pay attention to the roof gaps for Zyklon B.
Exiting the crematorium, you’ll see this guard tower.

After leaving the Gas Chamber and Crematorium I, walk through the nearby tunnel and go up the stairs to leave the grounds of the former concentration camp. If you are on a guided tour, please return your headset on the way out.

Near the gas chamber at Auschwitz you can see buildings used by the SS: garages, storages, offices, soldiers’ blocks of flats, Rudolf Höss villa, hospital and restaurant (notice the man on a barrel).
Blocks of flats for the SS are visible from the main carpark. The mass grave shown below is nearby.

is the Grave of the Last Victims of Auschwitz.
The so-called Judenrampe, roughly 500 m from Birkenau’s main gate, was the site of transport arrivals and selections between 1942 and 1944. The ruins of Auschwitz food warehouses are nearby.
After a break, you meet your guide and walk through the gate (
). You learn when and how this part of Auschwitz was built.
In the middle of the ramp, you learn about the conditions of deportation and selections of Jews. Then, walk on towards the distant trees.
Near the selection site you can see registration offices. The biggest one is
The cart by the kitchen in sector BIb was used to transport food.
Kitchen chimneys and water tank in sector BIId.
Along the Birkenau rail tracks, you pass two fire tanks (
) on the way to gas chamber ruins. In the background from left: registration office, a building being restored in the white tent, camp kitchen with many chimneys.
At the ramp end, between ruins of the biggest gas chambers and crematoriums, is a post-communist memorial to Auschwitz victims.
The largest drainage ditch was built by the penal unit which worked the hardest.
The Birkenau sewage plant was never completed, but its remains can still be seen around the crematorium ruins.
In the main office for registration newcomers were shaved, disinfected and got camp numbers. The photos in the last room were found in nearby
The 30 wooden warehouses, where property looted from the newcomers was sorted, almost completely burned down. However, some remnants can be seen at the site.
Crematoriums and Gas Chambers IV and V
Jews were told the gas chamber was a bathroom, so they undressed before entering. Later on, their corpses were burned, sometimes outdoors. Both events were photographed by Sonderkommando.
The biggest pond where human ashes were dumped is near the Sauna building, where you can see a metal cart used to transport the ashes. Close by there are Crematoriums IV and V ruins.

After visiting the sites above, you may return the same way or follow the road between the BII sectors and BIII, known as 'Mexico'. The path leads either through BIIa to the main gate or between BIIc and BIId to the selection site.
Near Birkenau gate, you can see the living and sanitary conditions in wooden stables.
Inside of a male barrack in Birkenau
On the way back from the ruins you can see the living and sanitary conditions in brick barracks. Take a virtual tour of:
You can see the washroom and toilets in the last row of smaller brick barracks.
Women held in the brick barracks left for work through a nearby gate—today, visitors exit Birkenau the same way.
Exit through the gate on the right near brick barracks. Then go left to the bus stop or the parking lot.

Auschwitz FAQ: Answers to the Most Common Visitor Questions

Planning a visit to Auschwitz-Birkenau? Below you'll find answers to the most frequently asked questions — from tickets and transport to dress code and what to expect on site. This guide is written by a licensed Auschwitz educator with over 20 years of experience, updated for 2026.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz free to visit?

children.text: Yes — entry to the Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial grounds is free, but only in the afternoon. You need a timed entry pass with  reserved online at visit.auschwitz.org. Guided educator tours cost extra (~130 PLN per person for 3.5 hours).

accordionItems.question: How do I book Auschwitz tickets?

children.text: Reserve at visit.auschwitz.org up to 90 days in advance. Choose between a free individual pass (afternoon slots) or a paid guided tour. Since March 2026, all passes are online-only — no on-site ticket sales. Reserve at least 2–3 weeks ahead, especially April through October.

accordionItems.question: What if Auschwitz tickets are sold out for my date?

children.text: Try different time slots or dates — cancellations appear daily. Check the afternoon free-entry window, which often has availability. Avoid third-party resellers who mark up prices for what is a free or low-cost reservation.

accordionItems.question: Can I buy Auschwitz tickets on the day?

children.text: No. Since March 1, 2026, on-site ticket sales have been permanently discontinued. All entry passes must be reserved online in advance at visit.auschwitz.org.

accordionItems.question: How much does a guided Auschwitz tour cost?

children.text: A 3.5-hour general guided tour costs ~130 PLN per person (120 PLN discounted for students under 26, seniors over 75, and disability card holders). A 6-hour study tour is ~170 PLN. Private tours for groups start at 870 PLN per group (as low as ~34 PLN per person for 30 people). Polish-language tours are 10–20 PLN cheaper. Full price lists:

accordionItems.question: Are third-party Auschwitz tickets legitimate?

children.text: Only reserve from the official visit.auschwitz.org. Third-party resellers often overcharge for what is a free or low-cost reservation. Some scammers sell supposedly "sold out" dates that are actually available, or provide only transport without actual museum entry.

accordionItems.question: How far in advance should I book Auschwitz?

children.text: At least 2–3 weeks ahead. Slots open 90 days before the visit date. Summer months (June–August) and weekends sell out fastest. For free afternoon passes, 7+ days ahead is usually sufficient.

accordionItems.question: Can I skip the line at Auschwitz?

children.text: Individual visitors and groups of up to 10 people can bypass the longest queues. A private tour lets you start 30 minutes before group tours begin. Visiting in the off-season (November–March) or early morning also means shorter wait times.

accordionItems.question: How do I get to Auschwitz from Kraków?

children.text: The Memorial is in Oświęcim, ~70 km west of Krakow. Options: direct Lajkonik bus from MDA station (~1.5h, 22 PLN), train (~1h, 18 PLN), car via A4 motorway (~1h), or organized transfer with tour included.

accordionItems.question: How far is Auschwitz from Krakow?

children.text: About 70 km by road, roughly 1–1.5 hours depending on transport. By train approximately 1 hour, by bus about 1.5 hours, by car around 1 hour via the A4 motorway.

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz from Warsaw?

children.text: Yes, but it is a long day. Warsaw to Oświęcim is ~350 km. Take a train to Krakow first (2.5 hours), then continue to Oświęcim. Alternatively, direct organized tours from Warsaw are available. Allow a full day.

accordionItems.question: Is there parking at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes — paid parking at both sites. Main lot at Auschwitz I: 20 PLN per car, 40 PLN per coach, 90 PLN per camper, 15 PLN per motorcycle. A free lot is available 700m away. Birkenau also has dedicated parking.

accordionItems.question: How do I get between Auschwitz I and Birkenau?

children.text: A free shuttle bus runs every 10–15 minutes between the two sites (3.5 km apart, ~8 minute ride). Check timetables at the bus stops. You can also walk (~40 min) or take a taxi (40 PLN).

accordionItems.question: How to get around the Museum and the city?

children.text: in Oświęcim is well connected to the Auschwitz Memorial and
and public transport to get around the area.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz located in Krakow?

children.text: No. The Memorial is in Oświęcim (historically known as Auschwitz in German), a separate town 70 km west of Kraków. Most visitors travel from Kraków as a day trip.

accordionItems.question: How long does an Auschwitz visit take?

children.text: A guided tour lasts 3.5 hours covering both sites. For a thorough self-guided visit, plan at least 4–5 hours. A study tour takes 6 or 8 hours. Including transport from Krakow, expect a at least half a day (6+ hours total).

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz without a guide?

children.text: Yes. Free individual entry passes are available for afternoon slots (for up to 10 people). You explore at your own pace using outdoor information boards. Preparation is essential — there is no audio guide provided. A guided tour is recommended for first-time visitors.

accordionItems.question: Is a self-guided Auschwitz visit worth it?

children.text: It depends on your preferences. Self-guided gives you more time for reflection and freedom to explore beyond the standard route. However, you miss the educator's context and stories. If you prepare well — read history, use our tour page walkthrough — a self-guided visit can be deeply meaningful. First-timers generally benefit more from a guided tour.

accordionItems.question: What is the difference between a general tour and a study tour?

children.text: A general tour lasts 3.5 hours and covers the main exhibits at both camp sites. A study tour lasts 6–8 hours with smaller groups, covering additional areas like the "Canada" sorting warehouses, registration building ("Sauna"), and more barracks. Study tours are recommended for those wanting deeper understanding.

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz and the Salt Mines on the same day?

children.text: Technically possible but rushed and very tiring. You would need to reserve an early morning Auschwitz slot and an afternoon Salt Mine visit. Organized combo tours exist but often feel cramped — groups arriving at the same time cause long queues. If possible, visit on separate days to properly absorb both experiences.

accordionItems.question: Is there an audio guide at Auschwitz?

children.text: There is no audio guide system at the Memorial. During guided tours, you receive a headset to hear your educator's narration (bring your own earphones with a 3.5mm jack). For self-guided visits, use the site's information boards and prepare beforehand.

accordionItems.question: What is the Auschwitz dress code?

children.text: Wear modest, respectful attire appropriate for a memorial site. No offensive or provocative clothing. Comfortable walking shoes are essential — much of the visit is outdoors on uneven terrain. Dress for the weather: summers can be hot, winters well below zero.
Complete dress code and seasonal guide

accordionItems.question: Can you take photos at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes, personal photography is allowed in most areas. Exceptions: no photography in the Block 4 victims' hair exhibit and Block 11 basements. No flash photography indoors, no tripods, selfie sticks, or recording of guided lectures. Be respectful — this is a memorial site.

accordionItems.question: What is the Auschwitz bag size limit?

children.text: Bags must not exceed 30×20×10 cm (roughly a small handbag). Larger bags, backpacks, and suitcases must be left in the free lockers at the visitor centre. This is checked at the airport-style security entrance.

accordionItems.question: What is not allowed at Auschwitz?

children.text: Prohibited items include: large bags and backpacks, alcohol, drugs, weapons (knives, scissors), sharp objects, drones, pets (except certified guide dogs), tripods, loud audio devices, political or religious or nationalistic flags, banners, and symbols. Eating and smoking are not allowed inside the grounds.

accordionItems.question: What should I bring to Auschwitz?

children.text: Required: your entry pass (printed or digital with barcode) and valid photo ID. Recommended: water bottle, umbrella or rain jacket, comfortable walking shoes, warm layers in colder months. Keep belongings minimal due to bag size restrictions (30×20×10 cm).

accordionItems.question: Is there luggage storage at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes — free lockers are available at the visitor centre for oversized bags and luggage. You can store items before entering and collect them after your visit.

accordionItems.question: What is the difference between Auschwitz and Birkenau?

children.text: They are two parts of one memorial complex, 3 km apart. Auschwitz I is the original camp — brick barracks housing museum exhibitions with preserved evidence and photographs. Auschwitz II-Birkenau is the much larger extermination camp with brick as well as wooden barracks, the selection ramp, and gas chamber ruins. Both are essential to visit. A free shuttle connects them.

accordionItems.question: What should I expect at Auschwitz?

children.text: A deeply moving experience. You will walk through the original camp grounds, authentic barracks, and preserved evidence of mass murder. Expect airport-style security at entry, then a solemn walk through exhibitions. Most of the visit is outdoors. The experience is emotionally intense — there is no "right" way to react. Allow yourself time and space afterward.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz appropriate for children?

children.text: The Museum recommends visitors be at least 14 years old. Children under 14 are allowed but must be accompanied by an adult at all times. Baby carriers are required — strollers cannot be used indoors. Parents should use their judgement based on their child's maturity.

accordionItems.question: How should I prepare emotionally for visiting Auschwitz?

children.text: Read basic history beforehand so you understand the context of what you will see. Consider watching a documentary or reading a survivor memoir (Primo Levi, Elie Wiesel, Edith Eger). Be prepared for difficult imagery — photographs of victims, and physical evidence (e.g. personal belongings). Give yourself quiet time after the visit. Avoid fictionalized accounts that distort the reality of the camp.

accordionItems.question: Is visiting Auschwitz worth it?

children.text: Yes. It is one of the most important memorial sites in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Visitors consistently describe it as profoundly moving and essential for understanding history. As a licensed guide since 2006, I believe everyone who is able to visit should do so at least once. Over 2.3 million visitors in 2025 alone proved it remains one of the most powerful historical experiences in Europe.

accordionItems.question: What should I read before visiting Auschwitz?

children.text: Start with Primo Levi's "If This Is a Man" — one of the most important Auschwitz testimonies. "Night" by Elie Wiesel and "The Choice" by Edith Eger are also highly recommended. This page provides a location-by-location walkthrough with historical context that will help you understand what you see on site:

accordionItems.question: What are the Auschwitz opening hours?

children.text: The Museum opens at 7:30 year-round. Closing time varies by month: 14:00 (December), 15:00 (January and November), 16:00 (February), 17:00 (March and October), 18:00 (April and September), 19:00 (May through August). Last entry is 90 minutes before closing. Closed: January 1, December 25, Easter Sunday.

accordionItems.question: When is the best time to visit Auschwitz?

children.text: For fewer crowds: visit November through March, or arrive at 8am or late in the day. Best weather with manageable crowds: April–June and September–October. Summer (July–August) has the longest hours but is the most crowded and hot. Individual visitors and groups up to 10 can bypass the long entry queue.

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz in winter?

children.text: Yes — the Museum is open year-round (except January 1, December 25, Easter Sunday). Winter visits are quieter with significantly fewer crowds. Dress very warmly — temperatures can drop well below zero, and much of the visit is outdoors. Hours are shorter (closing at 14:00–15:00 in December–January). Winter visits can feel particularly solemn and reflective.

accordionItems.question: Are there toilets and food at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes. Restrooms are available at the visitor centre and at several points in both sites. Restaurants operate at the Auschwitz I visitor centre and at Birkenau parking lot. Eating is permitted inside the camp grounds themselves but non-alcoholic drinks are allowed. Restaurants and shops are within 1 km of the main parking lot.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz wheelchair accessible?

children.text: Partially. Auschwitz I has no ramps for any exhibition buildings and all have stairs at entrance. Birkenau has challenging terrain — uneven surfaces, gravel, and grass — which can be difficult for wheelchair users. Wheelchairs are available at the Memorial but using them inside Auschwitz I buildings is against regulations due to the risk involved. Special entry is available with a disability ID. Guide dogs are permitted with certificates.

accordionItems.question: What languages are guided tours available in?

children.text: Group tours are available in Czech, English, French, German, Italian, Polish, Russian, Slovak, and Spanish. Private tours offer even more languages including Croatian, Dutch, Greek, Hebrew, Hungarian, Japanese, Korean, Serbian, Swedish, and Ukrainian — 19 languages total. Polish and English tours are the most frequent.

accordionItems.question: Where can I eat near Auschwitz?

children.text: A restaurant is near the Auschwitz I visitor centre entrance and cafeteria is inside it, another restaurant is at the Birkenau main parking lot. Additional restaurants, a supermarket, and cafés are within 1 km of the Museum. In Oświęcim town (1.5 km away), you will find more dining options around the market square. Pack water and a snack for the visit itself — no eating inside the grounds.

accordionItems.question: What documents do I need for Auschwitz entry?

children.text: A valid photo ID (passport, national ID card, or driver's license) and your entry pass (printed or shown digitally with barcode). Your pass is personalized — the name must match your ID.

accordionItems.question: How many people were murdered at Auschwitz?

children.text: Approximately 1.1 million people were murdered at Auschwitz-Birkenau. The vast majority — about 960,000 — were Jews from across Europe. Other victims included approximately 74,000 Poles, 21,000 Roma, 15,000 Soviet prisoners of war, and thousands of others. It was the deadliest single site of the Holocaust.

accordionItems.question: What happened at Auschwitz?

children.text: Auschwitz-Birkenau was the largest Nazi German concentration and extermination camp, operated from 1940 to 1945. It served multiple purposes: forced labour, human experimentation, and systematic mass murder. It was the site of the largest mass extermination in a single location in human history. Today it is preserved as a memorial and museum, and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

accordionItems.question: Who liberated Auschwitz?

children.text: The Soviet Red Army liberated Auschwitz on January 27, 1945. They found approximately 7,000 surviving prisoners. This date — January 27 — is now observed worldwide as International Holocaust Remembrance Day.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz a museum or a concentration camp?

children.text: Both. The original camp site is preserved as the Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum and Memorial, established in 1947 by the Polish Parliament. It is both a museum with permanent exhibitions and an authentic historical site — the preserved remains of the actual camp. It became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979.

accordionItems.question: What is the difference between Auschwitz I and Auschwitz II?

children.text: Auschwitz I (the "main camp") opened in 1940 in converted Polish army barracks. It served as the administrative centre and houses today's main exhibitions. Auschwitz II-Birkenau construction started in 1941 and was never concluded, was vastly larger — designed specifically as an extermination centre with biggest gas chambers and crematoria. Most of the killing took place at Birkenau.

accordionItems.question: How much does a guided Auschwitz tour cost?

children.text: A 3.5-hour general guided tour costs ~130 PLN per person (120 PLN discounted for students under 26, seniors over 75, and disability card holders). A 6-hour study tour is ~170 PLN. Private tours for groups start at 870 PLN per group (as low as ~34 PLN per person for 30 people). Polish-language tours are 10–20 PLN cheaper. Full price lists:

accordionItems.question: Are third-party Auschwitz tickets legitimate?

children.text: Only reserve from the official visit.auschwitz.org. Third-party resellers often overcharge for what is a free or low-cost reservation. Some scammers sell supposedly "sold out" dates that are actually available, or provide only transport without actual museum entry.

accordionItems.question: How far in advance should I book Auschwitz?

children.text: At least 2–3 weeks ahead. Slots open 90 days before the visit date. Summer months (June–August) and weekends sell out fastest. For free afternoon passes, 7+ days ahead is usually sufficient.

accordionItems.question: Can I skip the line at Auschwitz?

children.text: Individual visitors and groups of up to 10 people can bypass the longest queues. A private tour lets you start 30 minutes before group tours begin. Visiting in the off-season (November–March) or early morning also means shorter wait times.

accordionItems.question: How do I get to Auschwitz from Kraków?

children.text: The Memorial is in Oświęcim, ~70 km west of Krakow. Options: direct Lajkonik bus from MDA station (~1.5h, 22 PLN), train (~1h, 18 PLN), car via A4 motorway (~1h), or organized transfer with tour included.

accordionItems.question: How far is Auschwitz from Krakow?

children.text: About 70 km by road, roughly 1–1.5 hours depending on transport. By train approximately 1 hour, by bus about 1.5 hours, by car around 1 hour via the A4 motorway.

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz from Warsaw?

children.text: Yes, but it is a long day. Warsaw to Oświęcim is ~350 km. Take a train to Krakow first (2.5 hours), then continue to Oświęcim. Alternatively, direct organized tours from Warsaw are available. Allow a full day.

accordionItems.question: Is there parking at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes — paid parking at both sites. Main lot at Auschwitz I: 20 PLN per car, 40 PLN per coach, 90 PLN per camper, 15 PLN per motorcycle. A free lot is available 700m away. Birkenau also has dedicated parking.

accordionItems.question: How do I get between Auschwitz I and Birkenau?

children.text: A free shuttle bus runs every 10–15 minutes between the two sites (3.5 km apart, ~8 minute ride). Check timetables at the bus stops. You can also walk (~40 min) or take a taxi (40 PLN).

accordionItems.question: How to get around the Museum and the city?

children.text: in Oświęcim is well connected to the Auschwitz Memorial and
and public transport to get around the area.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz located in Krakow?

children.text: No. The Memorial is in Oświęcim (historically known as Auschwitz in German), a separate town 70 km west of Kraków. Most visitors travel from Kraków as a day trip.

accordionItems.question: How long does an Auschwitz visit take?

children.text: A guided tour lasts 3.5 hours covering both sites. For a thorough self-guided visit, plan at least 4–5 hours. A study tour takes 6 or 8 hours. Including transport from Krakow, expect a at least half a day (6+ hours total).

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz without a guide?

children.text: Yes. Free individual entry passes are available for afternoon slots (for up to 10 people). You explore at your own pace using outdoor information boards. Preparation is essential — there is no audio guide provided. A guided tour is recommended for first-time visitors.

accordionItems.question: Is a self-guided Auschwitz visit worth it?

children.text: It depends on your preferences. Self-guided gives you more time for reflection and freedom to explore beyond the standard route. However, you miss the educator's context and stories. If you prepare well — read history, use our tour page walkthrough — a self-guided visit can be deeply meaningful. First-timers generally benefit more from a guided tour.

accordionItems.question: What is the difference between a general tour and a study tour?

children.text: A general tour lasts 3.5 hours and covers the main exhibits at both camp sites. A study tour lasts 6–8 hours with smaller groups, covering additional areas like the "Canada" sorting warehouses, registration building ("Sauna"), and more barracks. Study tours are recommended for those wanting deeper understanding.

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz and the Salt Mines on the same day?

children.text: Technically possible but rushed and very tiring. You would need to reserve an early morning Auschwitz slot and an afternoon Salt Mine visit. Organized combo tours exist but often feel cramped — groups arriving at the same time cause long queues. If possible, visit on separate days to properly absorb both experiences.

accordionItems.question: Is there an audio guide at Auschwitz?

children.text: There is no audio guide system at the Memorial. During guided tours, you receive a headset to hear your educator's narration (bring your own earphones with a 3.5mm jack). For self-guided visits, use the site's information boards and prepare beforehand.

accordionItems.question: What is the Auschwitz dress code?

children.text: Wear modest, respectful attire appropriate for a memorial site. No offensive or provocative clothing. Comfortable walking shoes are essential — much of the visit is outdoors on uneven terrain. Dress for the weather: summers can be hot, winters well below zero.
Complete dress code and seasonal guide

accordionItems.question: Can you take photos at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes, personal photography is allowed in most areas. Exceptions: no photography in the Block 4 victims' hair exhibit and Block 11 basements. No flash photography indoors, no tripods, selfie sticks, or recording of guided lectures. Be respectful — this is a memorial site.

accordionItems.question: What is the Auschwitz bag size limit?

children.text: Bags must not exceed 30×20×10 cm (roughly a small handbag). Larger bags, backpacks, and suitcases must be left in the free lockers at the visitor centre. This is checked at the airport-style security entrance.

accordionItems.question: What is not allowed at Auschwitz?

children.text: Prohibited items include: large bags and backpacks, alcohol, drugs, weapons (knives, scissors), sharp objects, drones, pets (except certified guide dogs), tripods, loud audio devices, political or religious or nationalistic flags, banners, and symbols. Eating and smoking are not allowed inside the grounds.

accordionItems.question: What should I bring to Auschwitz?

children.text: Required: your entry pass (printed or digital with barcode) and valid photo ID. Recommended: water bottle, umbrella or rain jacket, comfortable walking shoes, warm layers in colder months. Keep belongings minimal due to bag size restrictions (30×20×10 cm).

accordionItems.question: Is there luggage storage at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes — free lockers are available at the visitor centre for oversized bags and luggage. You can store items before entering and collect them after your visit.

accordionItems.question: What is the difference between Auschwitz and Birkenau?

children.text: They are two parts of one memorial complex, 3 km apart. Auschwitz I is the original camp — brick barracks housing museum exhibitions with preserved evidence and photographs. Auschwitz II-Birkenau is the much larger extermination camp with brick as well as wooden barracks, the selection ramp, and gas chamber ruins. Both are essential to visit. A free shuttle connects them.

accordionItems.question: What should I expect at Auschwitz?

children.text: A deeply moving experience. You will walk through the original camp grounds, authentic barracks, and preserved evidence of mass murder. Expect airport-style security at entry, then a solemn walk through exhibitions. Most of the visit is outdoors. The experience is emotionally intense — there is no "right" way to react. Allow yourself time and space afterward.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz appropriate for children?

children.text: The Museum recommends visitors be at least 14 years old. Children under 14 are allowed but must be accompanied by an adult at all times. Baby carriers are required — strollers cannot be used indoors. Parents should use their judgement based on their child's maturity.

accordionItems.question: How should I prepare emotionally for visiting Auschwitz?

children.text: Read basic history beforehand so you understand the context of what you will see. Consider watching a documentary or reading a survivor memoir (Primo Levi, Elie Wiesel, Edith Eger). Be prepared for difficult imagery — photographs of victims, and physical evidence (e.g. personal belongings). Give yourself quiet time after the visit. Avoid fictionalized accounts that distort the reality of the camp.

accordionItems.question: Is visiting Auschwitz worth it?

children.text: Yes. It is one of the most important memorial sites in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Visitors consistently describe it as profoundly moving and essential for understanding history. As a licensed guide since 2006, I believe everyone who is able to visit should do so at least once. Over 2.3 million visitors in 2025 alone proved it remains one of the most powerful historical experiences in Europe.

accordionItems.question: What should I read before visiting Auschwitz?

children.text: Start with Primo Levi's "If This Is a Man" — one of the most important Auschwitz testimonies. "Night" by Elie Wiesel and "The Choice" by Edith Eger are also highly recommended. This page provides a location-by-location walkthrough with historical context that will help you understand what you see on site:

accordionItems.question: What are the Auschwitz opening hours?

children.text: The Museum opens at 7:30 year-round. Closing time varies by month: 14:00 (December), 15:00 (January and November), 16:00 (February), 17:00 (March and October), 18:00 (April and September), 19:00 (May through August). Last entry is 90 minutes before closing. Closed: January 1, December 25, Easter Sunday.

accordionItems.question: When is the best time to visit Auschwitz?

children.text: For fewer crowds: visit November through March, or arrive at 8am or late in the day. Best weather with manageable crowds: April–June and September–October. Summer (July–August) has the longest hours but is the most crowded and hot. Individual visitors and groups up to 10 can bypass the long entry queue.

accordionItems.question: Can I visit Auschwitz in winter?

children.text: Yes — the Museum is open year-round (except January 1, December 25, Easter Sunday). Winter visits are quieter with significantly fewer crowds. Dress very warmly — temperatures can drop well below zero, and much of the visit is outdoors. Hours are shorter (closing at 14:00–15:00 in December–January). Winter visits can feel particularly solemn and reflective.

accordionItems.question: Are there toilets and food at Auschwitz?

children.text: Yes. Restrooms are available at the visitor centre and at several points in both sites. Restaurants operate at the Auschwitz I visitor centre and at Birkenau parking lot. Eating is permitted inside the camp grounds themselves but non-alcoholic drinks are allowed. Restaurants and shops are within 1 km of the main parking lot.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz wheelchair accessible?

children.text: Partially. Auschwitz I has no ramps for any exhibition buildings and all have stairs at entrance. Birkenau has challenging terrain — uneven surfaces, gravel, and grass — which can be difficult for wheelchair users. Wheelchairs are available at the Memorial but using them inside Auschwitz I buildings is against regulations due to the risk involved. Special entry is available with a disability ID. Guide dogs are permitted with certificates.

accordionItems.question: What languages are guided tours available in?

children.text: Group tours are available in Czech, English, French, German, Italian, Polish, Russian, Slovak, and Spanish. Private tours offer even more languages including Croatian, Dutch, Greek, Hebrew, Hungarian, Japanese, Korean, Serbian, Swedish, and Ukrainian — 19 languages total. Polish and English tours are the most frequent.

accordionItems.question: Where can I eat near Auschwitz?

children.text: A restaurant is near the Auschwitz I visitor centre entrance and cafeteria is inside it, another restaurant is at the Birkenau main parking lot. Additional restaurants, a supermarket, and cafés are within 1 km of the Museum. In Oświęcim town (1.5 km away), you will find more dining options around the market square. Pack water and a snack for the visit itself — no eating inside the grounds.

accordionItems.question: What documents do I need for Auschwitz entry?

children.text: A valid photo ID (passport, national ID card, or driver's license) and your entry pass (printed or shown digitally with barcode). Your pass is personalized — the name must match your ID.

accordionItems.question: How many people were murdered at Auschwitz?

children.text: Approximately 1.1 million people were murdered at Auschwitz-Birkenau. The vast majority — about 960,000 — were Jews from across Europe. Other victims included approximately 74,000 Poles, 21,000 Roma, 15,000 Soviet prisoners of war, and thousands of others. It was the deadliest single site of the Holocaust.

accordionItems.question: What happened at Auschwitz?

children.text: Auschwitz-Birkenau was the largest Nazi German concentration and extermination camp, operated from 1940 to 1945. It served multiple purposes: forced labour, human experimentation, and systematic mass murder. It was the site of the largest mass extermination in a single location in human history. Today it is preserved as a memorial and museum, and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

accordionItems.question: Who liberated Auschwitz?

children.text: The Soviet Red Army liberated Auschwitz on January 27, 1945. They found approximately 7,000 surviving prisoners. This date — January 27 — is now observed worldwide as International Holocaust Remembrance Day.

accordionItems.question: Is Auschwitz a museum or a concentration camp?

children.text: Both. The original camp site is preserved as the Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum and Memorial, established in 1947 by the Polish Parliament. It is both a museum with permanent exhibitions and an authentic historical site — the preserved remains of the actual camp. It became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979.

accordionItems.question: What is the difference between Auschwitz I and Auschwitz II?

children.text: Auschwitz I (the "main camp") opened in 1940 in converted Polish army barracks. It served as the administrative centre and houses today's main exhibitions. Auschwitz II-Birkenau construction started in 1941 and was never concluded, was vastly larger — designed specifically as an extermination centre with biggest gas chambers and crematoria. Most of the killing took place at Birkenau.

meta.description: Expert answers on Auschwitz online booking, transport from Kraków, tour types, dress code, opening hours & what to expect — by a licensed guide since 2006.
